beats.text: Real datasets arrive with columns that are not numbers, and a model cannot multiply a weight by the string Berlin.
One-hot turns a column with K levels into K binary indicator columns, exactly one of which is hot per row.

visual.note: Every row becomes a unit vector pointing at its category

beats.text: A column with four distinct categories therefore adds exactly four indicator columns under plain one-hot encoding.
Ordinal encoding maps ranked levels to ascending integers, but applied to nominal data it invents an order the data never had.
One-hot is honest but scales with cardinality; a fifty-state column becomes fifty columns and a ZIP column tens of thousands.
Frequency encoding collapses K levels into one number, the count or share of each category, suiting high-cardinality columns and trees.

concepts.title: Target & WOE encoding — and how to keep them leakage-safe

beats.text: Target encoding replaces each category with the average target value for that category, one informative column regardless of cardinality.
Smoothing blends the category mean with the global prior, weighted by how often the category was actually seen.

visual.note: n_c rows in category c, m is smoothing strength

beats.text: With n_c equal to m equal to four, a rate of 0.75 and a prior of 0.5 blend exactly halfway to 0.6.

visual.label: smoothed encoded value at n_c = m = 4

beats.text: Weight of evidence replaces each category with the log-ratio of its positive share to its negative share.

visual.note: Lives on the log-odds scale logistic regression already speaks

beats.text: Fitting the encoding on the same rows you train on lets every row peek at its own answer, inflating scores that collapse in production.
Out-of-fold encoding computes each fold's category means from the other folds, so no row contributes to its own value.
Numeric columns still live on wildly different scales, so any distance or weighted-sum method lets large-magnitude columns dominate by accident.
Standardization subtracts the mean and divides by the standard deviation, centering each column at zero with unit variance.

visual.note: Number of standard deviations x sits from the mean

beats.text: Standardizing x equals eight with mean five and standard deviation three gives a z-score of exactly one.
Min-max linearly squeezes the column into the unit interval, mapping the minimum to zero and the maximum to one.

visual.note: Defined by the extremes, so one outlier crushes everyone

beats.text: Robust scaling centers on the median and divides by the interquartile range, so extreme values barely move either statistic.

visual.note: The median has a 50% breakdown point

beats.text: Trees split on thresholds within one feature, so scaling is wasted on them but is mandatory for k-NN, SVM, and neural nets.
Scaling moves and stretches a column but never changes its shape, so badly skewed features still fight many estimators.
The log transform compresses large values far more than small ones, taming the multiplicative spread of strictly positive data.
Box-Cox generalizes the log into a one-parameter family and lets maximum likelihood choose the exponent lambda.
At lambda equals one the transform reduces to x minus one, so applied to x equals two it returns exactly one.
Yeo-Johnson mirrors the power transform on each side of zero, extending the idea to the whole real line.

visual.note: No positivity constraint, no plus-constant hacks

beats.text: Choosing lambda by minimizing skewness traces a U-shaped curve whose bottom marks the most-Gaussian transform.
Binning chops a continuous variable into a few discrete intervals, trading resolution for robustness and interpretable score bands.
Equal-width cuts the value axis into k equal pieces, while equal-frequency cuts the data into k equally populated quantile piles.

visual.tex: e_j = x_{\min} + j\,\frac{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}{k}; \quad e_j = Q_{j/k}(x)
visual.note: Equal width versus equal frequency edges

beats.text: On skewed data, equal-width piles most points into one or two bins and leaves the rest nearly empty.
Equal-frequency keeps every bin equally populated, the safer default for skewed real-world columns.
Binning discards information and plants artificial discontinuities, so prefer splines or trees and reserve it for interpretability or regulation.
Four silent failures recur: leaking by fitting on full data, ordinal-coding nominal data, min-max with outliers, and unseen categories.

visual.label: ways encoding and scaling break a model
